title: Odd police video shows drone removing knife from motionless suspect
gist: A Sacramento County drone stunt disarming a motionless suspect highlights the rapidly expanding 'drone as first responder' market, with more than 1,800 US agencies now operating drones and vendors like Flock Safety, Axon, Skydio, Brinc and Motorola Solutions pitching surveillance-capable systems.

insights.claim: The EFF's Atlas of Surveillance lists more than 1,800 US police departments and sheriff's offices as having operated drones, with a notable rise in 'drone as first responder' programs in 2025.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Quantifies a large, growing public-sector demand base for law enforcement drone vendors.
insights.locator: The Electronic Frontier Foundation's Atlas of Surveillance

insights.claim: Companies pitching drone surveillance systems to law enforcement include Flock Safety, Axon, Skydio, Brinc and Motorola Solutions, with Flock's drones carrying its automated license plate readers.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Names the competitive vendor set for the law enforcement drone-surveillance market, several of which are public or VC-backed.
insights.locator: Such companies pitching drone surveillance systems

insights.claim: Sacramento County supervisors unanimously approved purchasing 27 additional drones at a starting price of $5,000 each as part of a larger $1 million package including a robot and a Bearcat armored vehicle.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Illustrates per-agency procurement budgets and unit economics ($5k/drone) feeding the law enforcement hardware market.

insights.claim: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office's 2025 inventory of 18 drones relied on Chinese manufacturers DJI and Autel, plus a single fixed-wing VTOL drone from Ohio-based Event 38.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Shows US law enforcement's continued dependence on Chinese drone hardware, a procurement and national-security exposure as restrictions on DJI loom.
insights.locator: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office listed 18 drones

insights.claim: EFF researcher Beryl Lipton warns drone-as-first-responder programs capture footage of homes, backyards and movements that require clear retention, audit, and use policies.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Privacy backlash and policy demands represent regulatory/reputational risk for the law enforcement drone sector.

insights.claim: Conscious armed suspects can defeat police drones, as shown when a man with a rifle shot down a Sacramento County drone in an October 2025 incident.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Tempers the operational utility of disarming drones, suggesting limited real-world applicability beyond incapacitated subjects.
